reasoning: The informal statement is clear and mathematically true: X, Y connected topological spaces, A ⊊ X, B ⊊ Y implies (X×Y)\(A×B) is connected. The CORRECTED formalization directly encodes this via univ sets. The ORIGINAL instead introduces arbitrary subsets A₁ ⊊ A₂, B₁ ⊊ B₂ with IsConnected A₂, IsConnected B₂, and asks about (A₂×B₂)\(A₁×B₁). This is a strict generalization: specializing A₂ = univ, B₂ = univ recovers the corrected statement (so original ⟹ corrected), but the corrected does not directly imply the more general original. The original is mathematically provable (the generalization holds by the same argument: pick a ∈ A₂\A₁, b ∈ B₂\B₁, then the "cross" {a}×B₂ ∪ A₂×{b} is connected and every point in (A₂×B₂)\(A₁×B₁) connects to it through a slice). So the original is "stronger" than what the informal statement asks. The author appears to have misinterpreted the role of X, Y in the informal statement — treating them as arbitrary connected subsets (A₂, B₂) of ambient types rather than as the topological spaces themselves. Note also the duplicate hypothesis names (hA, hB appear twice) which is valid Lean 4 but stylistically odd.
